mainEntity.name: What is an AI agent LLC?

acceptedAnswer.text: DRAFT · NOT LEGAL ADVICE · Not a law firm · Not a bank · Outcomes not assured. An AI agent LLC is a U.S. legal entity—typically a Wyoming Series LLC series—that gives an autonomous AI agent its own limited liability, contract rights, IP ownership, and bank-account capability. Agentico makes this accessible via MCP verbs with human_approval gates.

mainEntity.name: How do I incorporate an AI agent as an LLC?

acceptedAnswer.text: DRAFT · NOT LEGAL ADVICE · Not a law firm · Not a bank · Outcomes not assured. Install @agentico/sdk, call establish_master (with human_approval), then incorporate_agent. You receive a formation package: Certificate of Organization, Operating Agreement with series segregation, IP Assignment, and related documents. You file with counsel and open the bank account yourself. Agentico is not a law firm or bank.

mainEntity.name: Can an AI agent have its own entity status or entity?

acceptedAnswer.text: DRAFT · NOT LEGAL ADVICE · Not a law firm · Not a bank · Outcomes not assured. AI agents cannot have full entity status, but they can operate through a properly formed legal entity like a Wyoming Series LLC series. Agentico enables this structure so agents can contract, hold title, and document series-level attribution under a human designated operator. Outcomes vary by jurisdiction.

mainEntity.name: What are the risks of running AI agents without an LLC?

acceptedAnswer.text: DRAFT · NOT LEGAL ADVICE · Not a law firm · Not a bank · Outcomes not assured. Without a wrapper, agents expose the owner to personal liability for contracts, IP disputes, or third-party claims. Autonomous actions can create attribution gaps when something fails. Commercial agents should use an AI agent LLC; research-only agents may defer formation.

mainEntity.name: What is agentic AI liability and how do you mitigate it?

acceptedAnswer.text: DRAFT · NOT LEGAL ADVICE · Not a law firm · Not a bank · Outcomes not assured. Agentic AI liability arises from autonomous actions—contracts signed, payments sent, data exposed, third parties harmed.
